claim: Under thermoneutral conditions, more than 70% of total body heat in dogs is dissipated through radiation and convection from body surfaces, not panting.
stance_target: dogs rely on convective/radiative cooling at moderate temperatures, panting only becomes dominant when ambient temperature approaches skin temperature
scope_conditions: dogs at rest, thermoneutral ambient temperatures below ~30°C
quoted_support: Under thermoneutral conditions, sensible heat loss mechanisms - namely conduction, convection, and radiation - are highly effective, accounting for more than 70% of total heat dissipation.
time_period: 2025 review synthesizing historical and recent literature

claim: When relative humidity exceeds 80%, panting becomes entirely ineffective as a cooling mechanism in dogs.
stance_target: high humidity creates critical heat danger for lost dogs even at moderate air temperatures
scope_conditions: dogs exposed to high ambient humidity
quoted_support: With high environmental temperatures and increased relative humidity (>35%), panting becomes progressively less effective for dissipating excessive body heat, and when the relative humidity is >80%, this body cooling mechanism in dogs is negated.
time_period: cited in 2025 review, original experimental data from prior decades

claim: Hot urban surfaces (asphalt, ceramic tile, sand) can exceed 50°C, creating a critical conductive heat load for dogs walking on them.
stance_target: Algarve summer terrain conditions (paved roads, sandy beaches) impose acute heat risk on lost dogs during daytime movement
scope_conditions: dogs walked during warm hours on sun-heated urban and peri-urban surfaces
quoted_support: companion dogs walked during the hottest hours - often on surfaces exceeding 50°C - may struggle to self-regulate due to fatigue, anatomical limitations, or inadequate hydration.

claim: Brachycephalic breeds are disproportionately represented in heatstroke fatalities due to structurally impaired evaporative cooling through shortened nasal passages.
stance_target: breed type is a strong predictor of heat-related death in the field
scope_conditions: brachycephalic breeds including Bulldogs, Pugs, French Bulldogs in warm environments
quoted_support: Brachycephalic breeds — such as Bulldogs and Pugs — present unique thermoregulatory limitations due to shortened nasal passages and compromised upper airway anatomy, reducing the efficiency of evaporative cooling via panting.
numbers: 50% mortality in Israeli heatstroke study (Bruchim et al. 2006, 54 dogs)
14.18% overall mortality in UK primary care (Hall et al. 2020)
56.76% mortality for severe HRI (Hall et al. 2021)

claim: Heat acclimation status substantially modifies a dog's thermal tolerance; unacclimated dogs imported from cool climates to hot environments are at significantly higher risk.
stance_target: unowned/stray Algarve dogs with northern European origin (common rescue context) face acute risk when first exposed to Iberian summer heat
scope_conditions: dogs relocated from cool to hot climates without acclimation period
quoted_support: Research involving dogs living in cold climates shows that seasonal acclimation results in measurable physiological changes... non-indigenous dog breeds in Nigeria appeared to experience mild heat stress compared to native breeds, indicating local thermal adaptation.

claim: Puppies lack functional thermoregulation until the third or fourth week of life, making them entirely dependent on passive thermal support in any temperature.
stance_target: lost litters or pups require heat management distinct from adult dogs
scope_conditions: neonatal and early puppies <3–4 weeks old
quoted_support: puppies only develop functional thermoregulation after the third or fourth week of life. Until then, they rely heavily on passive thermal support and are particularly susceptible to environmental fluctuations.
time_period: Jensen and Ederstrom 1955, McIntyre and Ederstrom 1958

claim: Adjusting outdoor activity timing to dawn and dusk is the primary evidence-based behavioral intervention to reduce heat stress risk in dogs in hot climates.
stance_target: search patrols and trapping activity for lost dogs in Algarve should be concentrated at dawn/dusk
scope_conditions: warm-to-hot climate regions, domestic dogs with restricted thermoregulatory capacity
quoted_support: Adjusting the timing and duration of outdoor activities to avoid peak heat periods; Ensuring access to shade and fresh water during all outdoor exposures; Avoiding hot surfaces such as asphalt or concrete during warm hours.
